next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

At 4:13 a.m., Anika woke before the watch could buzz. Last night it had finally matched the microwave clock exactly, and now both green digits still read 4:13 while she lay awake waiting for the alarm she no longer needed. The apartment was dark, and she lay still long enough to hear three separate sounds from the building: pipes knocking two floors down, somebody's toilet tank refilling, the elevator cables whining through their own metal throat. Her body had learned this hour the way skin learns a scar. At this hour she used to think in a strict sequence: court time, water bottle, bandage tape, bus.

Then the fact landed again, blunt as a hand on the sternum. There was no court and no impact for six weeks. The restriction sheet on the fridge said `right tibia, stress fracture` in blue stamped letters.

She rolled onto her back and felt for pain before she moved, the way she had been doing since the hospital. The bone did not throb while she was still. It only answered when weight asked it a question. Humid air stuck to her neck, and she could not tell if she was sweating or just already awake enough to notice every part of herself. She flexed her toes under the sheet, testing, counting: one, two, three, four, five. She hated that she was counting before she had even sat up.

The hallway outside her room was seven and a half strides if she took normal steps and six if she made her hips turn fast like ghosting drills. She had measured it three nights ago. Tonight she waited until 4:31, because the rule on the notebook page said she had to try breathing first. I...
open_hooks_to_carry: Anika plays through sharp pain in decisive rally stretches.
Ravi gives an on-court signal to keep pushing.
prev_tail_excerpt:
It was not his step she waited for. She waited for the other one, the imaginary lighter one with the quick key-jingle and the plastic hospital tag tapping metal. She listened until her ears rang and the building settled and nothing happened.

She reached for her notebook on the floor.

She wrote the usual headers: date, time home, food.

Her pen hovered.

Then, underneath the usual columns, she wrote a sentence she had not written in months, the words uneven because her hand shook.

I laughed so hard a cookie fell into my shirt and everyone saw.

She stared at it. She did not cross it out.

Before turning off the lamp, she texted Zoe.

Home now. Dad just grounded me till qualifiers.

Three dots appeared immediately.

That's bullshit. I'm still here. Don't disappear again.

Anika typed I won't, deleted it, and replaced it with I'll try. She sent that version and hated how small it looked.

The watch on her wrist flashed 10:47. In five hours and fifty-three minutes, the 4:40 alarm would go off. Training would begin. Her shin would complain on the first lunges. Dad would track intervals on the legal pad. By six-thirty, they'd be under the Midtown lobby lights for qualifiers. The new nine p.m. curfew would lock her phone on the kitchen table tomorrow night whether she wanted it there or not.

All of that was still true.

She lay down anyway, frosting sugar on her tongue, party noise ghosting in her ears, and listened to her own anger breathing beside the old waiting at the door.
state_deltas_required: Qualify for regional pool at the city tournament.
Anika qualifies but collapses; imaging confirms a tibial stress fracture and mandates six weeks of no impact training.
